cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A global network of interconnected computing devices that communicate using standardized protocols, enabling data exchange worldwide

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A small chunk of data transmitted over a network — messages are broken into these units, each with a header containing source and destination information

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A system that translates human-readable domain names (like www.example.com) into numerical IP addresses

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The property of a network that allows it to continue functioning correctly even when some of its components fail

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The duplication of critical components or data paths in a system so that if one fails, another can take over

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing approach in which multiple processors perform separate sub-tasks simultaneously to solve a problem faster

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing model in which processing tasks are spread across multiple networked computers, often to tackle problems too large for a single machine

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The maximum rate at which data can be transmitted over a network connection, typically measured in bits per second

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A set of agreed-upon rules that govern how data is formatted and transmitted between devices on a network

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A unique numerical label assigned to each device connected to a network that uses the Internet Protocol for communication

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The version of Internet Protocol that uses 32-bit addresses, supporting about 4 billion unique addresses

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The version of Internet Protocol that uses 128-bit addresses, providing a vastly larger address space than its predecessor

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A network device that directs data packets from their source toward their destination by reading packet headers and forwarding along the best path

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing approach where tasks are performed one step at a time, in order, on a single processor

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A reliable, connection-oriented internet protocol that ensures all packets arrive and are reassembled in order, retransmitting any lost packets

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A connectionless internet protocol that sends packets without guaranteed delivery or ordering, used when speed is more important than reliability

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An internet outage takes down several routers in the middle of a network. Despite this, most users can still access websites. Which property of the internet best explains this?

choices.text: Fault tolerance — the internet is designed with multiple paths so packets can be rerouted around failed nodes
choices.explanation: The internet's decentralized, redundant design means packets find alternate paths when routers fail — this is fault tolerance by design.

choices.text: Bandwidth — the high bandwidth of the remaining connections compensates for the lost routers
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is about data transfer rate, not about surviving component failures — more bandwidth does not help if there are no paths to route data.

choices.text: DNS — the domain name system automatically reroutes traffic around failed nodes
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses — it does not control packet routing or compensate for failed routers.

choices.text: Sequential computing — each user's connection is handled one at a time, reducing demand on the network
choices.explanation: Sequential computing is unrelated to network fault tolerance — the internet handles millions of connections simultaneously, not sequentially.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A task that takes 100 seconds to run on a single processor. The task is divided into 4 equal independent subtasks. What is the minimum time needed if 4 processors run the subtasks in parallel?

choices.text: 25 seconds
choices.explanation: Each subtask takes 100/4 = 25 seconds. With 4 processors running them simultaneously, the total time is 25 seconds — a 4× speedup.

choices.text: 400 seconds
choices.explanation: 400 seconds would result if the processors ran tasks sequentially — parallel computing is specifically about reducing time by running simultaneously.

choices.text: 100 seconds
choices.explanation: 100 seconds is the original single-processor time — parallel computing should reduce the total time if tasks are truly independent.

choices.text: 4 seconds
choices.explanation: 4 seconds would require a 25× speedup — with 4 processors and 4 equal tasks, the maximum speedup is 4×, giving 25 seconds, not 4.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A user types 'www.collegeboard.org' into a browser. Which of the following correctly describes the role of DNS in what happens next?

choices.text: DNS translates the domain name into an IP address so the browser knows which server to contact
choices.explanation: DNS is the domain name system — it resolves human-readable names to machine-readable IP addresses, enabling the browser to route the request.

choices.text: DNS encrypts the user's request to protect it from interception
choices.explanation: Encryption is handled by HTTPS/TLS, not DNS — DNS focuses on name-to-IP resolution, not security.

choices.text: DNS breaks the request into packets for transmission across the internet
choices.explanation: Packet creation is handled by TCP/IP protocols — DNS only translates names to addresses, it does not handle packet formation.

choices.text: DNS checks whether the website is safe before allowing the browser to connect
choices.explanation: Security checking is done by browsers, antivirus software, or security services — DNS simply translates names to IP addresses without evaluating safety.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why the internet is considered to have a decentralized architecture?

choices.text: No single authority controls all the routers and servers; data can travel through many different paths between any two points
choices.explanation: The internet has no central governing machine — routing decisions are distributed across many independent nodes, which also provides fault tolerance.

choices.text: All data is stored on servers located in one central data center
choices.explanation: Centralized data storage is the opposite of decentralization — the internet specifically distributes data and routing across many independent systems.

choices.text: Internet connections are always faster when data takes the shortest possible path
choices.explanation: Shortest path is a routing optimization strategy, not what defines decentralization — a network can be centralized yet still optimize routes.

choices.text: The internet is decentralized because all computers must connect through one main server
choices.explanation: Requiring all connections through one server would be centralized architecture — the internet explicitly avoids single points of control or failure.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A video call app uses UDP instead of TCP for transmitting video frames. Which of the following best justifies this choice?

choices.text: UDP is faster because it does not require acknowledgment of received packets — a dropped frame is preferable to freezing the video waiting for retransmission
choices.explanation: Real-time video prioritizes speed over completeness — UDP's lack of retransmission means the call continues smoothly even if occasional frames are lost.

choices.text: UDP guarantees delivery of all packets, making it more reliable than TCP for important video data
choices.explanation: UDP does not guarantee delivery — TCP does. UDP is chosen despite this for real-time applications where low latency matters more than completeness.

choices.text: TCP is not capable of transmitting video data at all
choices.explanation: TCP can transmit any type of data — the choice of UDP is about performance trade-offs, not capability limitations of TCP.

choices.text: UDP uses encryption by default, protecting the video call from eavesdropping
choices.explanation: Neither UDP nor TCP encrypts data by default — encryption is a separate layer (like DTLS for UDP or TLS for TCP) independent of transport protocol choice.
